name: A Letter for the Unknown — First Algebra
whatIsIt: Sometimes we don't yet know a number. We use a box □ or a letter (like x) to stand for it. 'x + 3 = 10' asks: what number plus 3 makes 10? To find x, do the OPPOSITE operation: 10 − 3 = 7, so x = 7. A letter is just a placeholder for the mystery number until we work it out.
whyNeeded: Using a letter for an unknown is the single biggest doorway into higher maths — algebra, equations, formulas, and everything in science and engineering. It also mirrors real thinking: 'how many more do I need?', 'what number makes this true?'
realLifeUses: 'I have ₹40, the toy costs ₹65 — how much more do I need?' (40 + x = 65)
Sharing: '3 friends, 24 sweets, how many each?' (3 × x = 24)
A missing score needed to reach a total
Formulas like area = length × breadth use letters for any number

vocabulary.meaning: A letter or box that stands for an unknown number.
A statement that two things are equal, like x + 3 = 10.
Find the value of the unknown that makes the equation true.

story.title: The Missing Marbles
story.text: Vihaan had some marbles, won 5 more, and now has 12. 'How many did you start with?' asks Didi. Vihaan writes it as a puzzle: □ + 5 = 12. 'To undo the +5, I subtract: 12 − 5 = 7.' He checks: 7 + 5 = 12. 'I started with 7!' Didi grins — 'you just did algebra.'
story.extractedProblem: Vihaan won 5 marbles and now has 12. He started with □. What is □?

visual.caption: The whole bar is 12; the known part is 5; the unknown part x fills the rest: x = 12 − 5 = 7.

teachingGallery.title: Undo to find the unknown
teachingGallery.note: Do the opposite operation to free the letter.

standardMethod.summary: Write the situation as an equation with a letter, then do the OPPOSITE operation to get the letter alone.
steps: Let a letter (or □) stand for the unknown.
Write the equation from the words.
Do the OPPOSITE operation to both sides: + undoes −, × undoes ÷.
Check by putting your answer back into the equation.
The +6 is stuck to x; undo it with −6.
x is multiplied by 4; undo with ÷4.

commonMistakes.mistake: Doing the SAME operation instead of the opposite (adding 3 again to solve x + 3 = 10).
commonMistakes.fix: To free the letter, do the OPPOSITE: + is undone by −, × is undone by ÷.

commonMistakes.mistake: Not checking the answer back in the equation, so slips slip through.
commonMistakes.fix: Always substitute your answer back: it must make the equation true.

challenge.q: Riya has ₹x, spends ₹30, and is left with ₹45. How much did she start with?
Sam solves x + 7 = 20 by writing x = 27. What went wrong?

options.label: He added instead of subtracting — x = 20 − 7 = 13

challenge.answer: He added instead of subtracting — x = 20 − 7 = 13

questions.q: After solving an equation you should always…

options.label: Check by putting the answer back in

questions.answer: Check by putting the answer back in

teachBackPrompt: Make up a 'mystery number' puzzle for a grown-up (like '□ + 6 = 14'). Explain how you use a box or letter for the unknown and do the opposite operation to solve it — then check your answer together.

revisionCard.summary: A letter (or □) stands for an unknown number. Write the words as an equation, then do the OPPOSITE operation to free the letter: + undoes −, × undoes ÷. Always check by putting the answer back in. This is the doorway to algebra.

meta.curriculum: CBSE/NCERT Class 6 (introduction to algebra — variables & simple equations)

rememberIt.hook: A letter is just a box for an unknown number
rememberIt.unpack: x means 'the number we do not know yet'. 3x means 3 times x. Whatever you do to one side of '=', do to the other.

studentQuestions.q: Why use letters instead of numbers?
studentQuestions.a: A letter holds a number we do not know yet, so we can write a rule for ANY value. x is a placeholder.
3 times x. A number stuck to a letter means multiply - there is a hidden times sign.
